title: The Macro Minute: Is the US growth cycle helping or hurting the 42 Macro Paradigm C bull market?

Happy Tuesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Tuesday,
December 23rd, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. So, let's
dive right in. Today's key macro
question is, is the US growth cycle
helping or hurting the 42 macro paradigm
C bull market? The short answer is,
according to our macro weather model,
the tailwind from the growth cycle is by
far the biggest tailwind for risk assets
at present. We believe the tailwind will
accelerate in the quarters ahead and
continue to view consensus real GDP
estimates for 2026 to 2027 as being
roughly 50% too low at the current
juncture. Terms of analytical nuance and
boy is there a lot today. The Q3 GDP
October capex and durable goods and
November industrial production data
supported our resilient US economy theme
and the U-shaped economy thesis.
Unpacking the Q3 GDP data, the headline
growth dynamic supported our resilient
US economy theme, particularly the
strong positive impulses in real GDP and
real personal consumption expenditures.
The strong positive impulse in real
final sales to private domestic
purchases and weak positive impulse in
real final sales to public domestic
purchasers supported our resilient US
economy theme. The investment growth
dynamic supported our U-shaped economy
thesis, particularly the weak negative
impulse in real private fixed
investment, which slowed to 1%
quarter-over-arter saw in Q3, which was
the lowest since fourth quarter of 2024.
The tariff impact supported our U-shaped
economy thesis as well, and that it
signals headline real GDP growth is
slightly weaker than implied.
Specifically, the 45 basis point
contribution to headline real GDP from
imports and inventories in Q3 was 85
basis points u greater than the long run
mean of the time series. The strong
negative impulses in industrial
production and capacity utilization in
November support our view that the
resilient US economy is probing for a
bottom of its U-shaped slowdown in the
second half of 2025 as did the strong
positive impulse in core capital goods
new orders and weak negative impulse in
durable goods new orders. Reminder,
employment growth as measured by the
establishment survey was non-existent on
either a three-month or six-month
annualized basis in November,
particularly true private services
payrolls, which represent greater than
50% of total employment, 54% to be
exact. Reminder, the US labor market has
become substantially less dynamic during
the Trump 2.0 administration with
roughly 90% of all employment growth
coming from the government or government
adjacent sectors like healthcare and
social services. As such, the
reacelerations in NIPA corporate profits
growth to well above trend rates of 9.1%
year-over-year and 16.9%
quarter-over-arter SAR in Q3, which were
the highest since the fourth quarter of
2023 and fourth quarter of 2024,
respectively, support our view that the
resilient US economy is headed for a
jobless recovery that will perpetuate a
boom in corporate profits due to
sustained above trend productivity
growth.
Reminder, consensus is still too bearish
on aggregate output relative to our
resilient US economy and paradigm C
themes. Real GDP growth is likely to be
roughly 50% higher than consensus
currently anticipates throughout 2026
and 2027. With that bullish backdrop in
mind, we see that consensus is currently
not as bullish as we are on the
corporate profits outlook according to
the 22nd percentile reading of the S&P
500 next 12 month 12-month earnings per
share ratio. Cassentis may be as bullish
as we are on the US corporate profits
outlook, looking further ahead,
according to the 64th percentile reading
in the S&P 500 current fiscal year EPS
to next fiscal year EPS ratio. But the
risk of upside surprises over the next
few quarters remains elevated. And
lastly, the GDP deflator and core PC
price deflator supported our sticky
inflation theme and tariff pig in the
Python thesis. As always, members of our
global investor community can trust that
our institutional-grade risk management
overlays, Kiss and Dr. Mo, will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Uh, transition micro dashboard is always
a wrap up with a question from our
community. Actually, I just realized
a lot of economist gobbly go here. Let
me take off my economist hat. I'm
guessing half of you don't even know
what a weak positive impulse or strong
positive impulse or this or that means.
So, let me explain that. I apologize.
It's very uh technical. A strong
positive impulse is a a situation in in
a time series where the three-month
annualized rate of change is greater
than the six-month annualized rate of
change and the six-month annualized rate
of change is greater than the
year-over-year rate of change. So, what
that indicates is an acceleration in the
slope of the underlying time series.
Obviously, when you're talking about
growth, that's phenomenal. When you're
talking about inflation, not so much.
Um, a weak positive impulse means uh the
3-month annualized rate of change is
above the six-month annualized rate of
change, but the six-month annualized
related change is below the
year-over-year rate of change. So, it
suggests that there is a transitory, at
least for now, acceleration in the slope
of the underlying time series that is
not yet trending. Uh and conversely, a
strong negative impulse means the
three-month annualized rate of change is
below the six-month annualized rate of
change, which itself is below the
year-over-year rate of change. So, it
means you have a a a a trending
deceleration in the underlying slope of
the of the slope of the underlying time
series. And then conversely, a weak
negative impulse means the three-month
annualized rate of change is below the
six-month annualized rate of change, but
the six-month annualized rate of change
is above the year-over-year rate of
change. you have this transitory uh
deceleration at least for now uh uh in
the slope of the underlying uh time
series. So hopefully that uh could clear
some things up for you guys. You know,
as you guys know, I love educating. It's
a labor of love. My goal in life is to
transform the investing habits of the
general investing public. So that the
vin diagram of investing activity
according to institutional investors
like myself and the investing act other
circle the investing activity according
to retail investors merges over time.
It'll never be a cons you know just two
concentric circle. It'll never be, you
know, just one big circle cuz, you know,
retail investors don't have guardrails
in their process to prevent their
amydalas from being tickled by bull porn
purveyors or bare porn purveyors. And
so, they'll always be making more
mistakes on average at a higher
frequency to a greater severity than the
median institutional investor. But my
goal and the goal of 42 macro, the
company that me and my partners and our
and our and our teammates build is to
push those two circles together in the
direction of the investing activity of
the institutional investors uh that I'm
partner with across global Wall Street.

dashboard here as always I wrap up with

andor additionally contribute to the
imbalance in the treasury market that
has been good uh for gold. So uh

is because the explosive growth or you

want to call it in terms of the JGB uh
bond yields at 10ear yields in Japan are
the highest they've ever been as they've
been since I want to say 98 or 99.
Two-year yields in Japan are the highest
they've been since 1997. the increase
the durable increase in bond yields uh
in Japan as Japan has exited deflation.
If you look at Japan's CPI, it's been
somewhere at or bel ab above 3% for
three years now. So they've exited
deflation and so that that dynamic is
causing a reversal of the capital flow
dynamic from Japan to the US. Recall
that Japan is the US's second largest uh
foreign creditor from the perspective of
the marketable treasury debt securities
market which is somewhere around $30
trillion. Japan owns about I want to say
somewhere around um you know five to
six% of that of of that total and so now
that the Japanese capital allocators and
again Japan is one of the world's um
largest net international investment
surplus economy so that you know in
layman's terms Japan has all the money
alongside Germany alongside China um to
a lesser degree um to a lesser degree
Switzerland um but you know these are
the places with all the money in the in
the world in the global economy so if
you remove a source of demand from one
of the world's largest net international
investment creditors, i.e. Japan, which
just so happens to be one of our largest
uh uh uh capitalizers in terms of the
Treasury bond market, then you're you're
it puts upward bias on yields because
you're removing at the margins that
source of demand because the Japanese
investors can just stay home and
capitalize their bond market. In fact,
when you adjust for the cost effect of
FX hedging from the perspective of
Japanese investors who are allocating
assets to the Treasury market right now,
they're essentially picking up about a
negative 110 basis point drag from
investing in treasuries on a 10-year
basis and hedging them uh and rolling
those hedges forward right now. Um
roughly about 110 basis point drag
relative to what they would pick up by
just buying a 10-year JGB. In fact, I
was I hadn't looked at that uh this
week. It might be closer to 120 150
basis points just based on the uh uh uh
widening uh uh um a slope uh in the
Japanese uh uh yield curve. So, the
yield curve that that slope that widens,
it'll it'll start to uh uh cause that
that negative spread to to get even more
negative. So, uh you're basically not
getting paid to buy treasuries as a
Japanese investor right now if your goal
is to hedge out the currency risk. And
over time, as we move forward over the
next couple of years, we ultimately
think that Japanese investors will be
increasingly forced to hedge out their
currency risk as a partially as a
function of the uh structural uh uh uh
reform that we continue to call for and
expect out of the Federal Reserve that
should create the conditions for a much
lower uh US dollar on a long-term uh
basis. So, uh wrap it up there. Uh and

in Silicon Valley.

amounts of hardship that are being
levied upon those on the bottom of the
cave. This the the the low to median

income interest rate sensitive sectors

a better job of creating policy so that
we have a more cohesive and harmonious
